coredata.dc:title: Facile microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse decorated anatase-rutile TiO2 for efficient ciprofloxacin removal

coredata.dc:description: This study developed a facile and eco-friendly microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from sugarcane bagasse, integrated with a controlled anatase-rutile TiO₂ phase for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) in wastewater. The resulting CQDs/TiO₂ nanocomposites (NCs) exhibited superior optical properties, efficient charge separation, and extended light absorption, as confirmed by UV–Vis, XRD, EIS, BET, transient photocurrent response, PL, FTIR, TEM, and XPS analyses. Among the composites, 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs achieved the highest efficiency, degrading 99.91 % of CIP under UV irradiation (5 h, 20 ppm). The nanocomposites demonstrated notable stability, retaining 69 % degradation efficiency after five cycles, confirming their potential for long-term wastewater treatment. EIS and transient photocurrent response analyses showed that 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance and highest photocurrent, validating improved charge separation. Scavenger tests identified h+ as the dominant reactive species, with •OH also contributing, confirming hole-mediated oxidation as the primary mechanism. LC-MS/MS confirmed complete CIP mineralization, providing insights into degradation pathways. This study highlights the synergistic role of CQDs and the anatase-rutile interface in enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The microwave-assisted synthesis offers a scalable and sustainable approach, demonstrating strong potential for advanced wastewater treatment and environmental remediation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectra of CQDs under different conditions (inset: CQDs synthesized with 500 mg sugarcane carbon matrix at 950 W under UV and daylight); (b) UV–Vis spectra of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs; (c) Bandgap calculation by Tauc plot for CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (inset: TiO₂).
(a) PL spectra of as-synthesized CQDs in different conditions; (b) normalized PL spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 NCs.
XRD patterns of synthesized TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 NCs.

(a) TEM image of CQDs showing morphology, particle size distribution (inset), lattice fringes, and TEM-EDX mapping; (b) SEM image of TiO₂ depicting particle morphology and size distribution (inset); (c) HR-TEM image revealing lattice fringes of anatase-rutile TiO₂ phases.
HR-TEM, HAADF, and elemental mapping of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs.
FTIR spectra of CQDs, TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2 NCs.
XPS spectra of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs: (a) Ti 2p, (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2 NCs, (b) BJH pore size distribution.
EIS spectra (a) and transient photocurrent response (b) of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs with varying CQDs loadings.

Photocatalytic degradation of CIP under UV irradiation: (a) Pseudo-first-order kinetics (−ln C/C₀ vs. time), (b) CIP concentration decay (C/C₀ vs. time), (c) Photocatalytic performance comparison of various catalysts over 5 h, and (d) Reusability of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs over 5 cycles within 5 h.
LC MS/MS spectra of CIP solution after 300 min irradiation with CQDs/TiO2 as catalyst.
Schematic illustration of photocatalytic mechanism for CIP in the presence of CQDs/TiO2 NCs catalyst under UV irradiation.

head.ce:title: Facile microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse decorated anatase-rutile TiO2 for efficient ciprofloxacin removal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study developed a facile and eco-friendly microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from sugarcane bagasse, integrated with a controlled anatase-rutile TiO₂ phase for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) in wastewater. The resulting CQDs/TiO₂ nanocomposites (NCs) exhibited superior optical properties, efficient charge separation, and extended light absorption, as confirmed by UV–Vis, XRD, EIS, BET, transient photocurrent response, PL, FTIR, TEM, and XPS analyses. Among the composites, 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs achieved the highest efficiency, degrading 99.91 % of CIP under UV irradiation (5 h, 20 ppm). The nanocomposites demonstrated notable stability, retaining 69 % degradation efficiency after five cycles, confirming their potential for long-term wastewater treatment. EIS and transient photocurrent response analyses showed that 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance and highest photocurrent, validating improved charge separation. Scavenger tests identified h+ as the dominant reactive species, with •OH also contributing, confirming hole-mediated oxidation as the primary mechanism. LC-MS/MS confirmed complete CIP mineralization, providing insights into degradation pathways. This study highlights the synergistic role of CQDs and the anatase-rutile interface in enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The microwave-assisted synthesis offers a scalable and sustainable approach, demonstrating strong potential for advanced wastewater treatment and environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demand for clean water worldwide has intensified the need for efficient purification technologies to treat non-potable water and wastewater [1,2]. Among emerging contaminants, antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin (CIP) pose significant environmental and health concerns due to their persistent accumulation in aquatic ecosystems [3,4]. Widespread antibiotic usage in medical treatments, including gastrointestinal, urinary tract, dermatological, and pulmonary infections, has exacerbated the issue by promoting the proliferation of antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Conventional water treatment methods, such as biodegradation, membrane filtration, and adsorption, have proven insufficient for the complete removal of these pollutants [5]. This necessitates the development of advanced and highly efficient remediation strategies to ensure effective antibiotic degradation in wastewater.

Photocatalysis has emerged as a promising approach for wastewater treatment due to its efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and environmentally friendly nature [6]. Photocatalytic-based semiconductor materials have gained significant attention in recent years due to their cost-effective processing, high photocatalytic activity, and environmentally friendly nature, as they do not cause secondary pollution [7]. Among these materials, titanium dioxide (TiO₂) is one of the most widely studied photocatalysts due to its chemical stability, non-toxicity, and powerful oxidative capabilities. However, TiO₂ has two primary limitations: its dependence on UV light for activation and the rapid recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, both of which limit its photocatalytic efficiency. Overcoming these challenges requires material modifications that enhance charge separation and broaden the light absorption spectrum. Despite extensive research on TiO₂ photocatalysis, its practical application remains limited due to inefficient charge transfer and narrow spectral absorption. While previous studies have explored carbon-based nanomaterials to enhance TiO₂ performance, the use of sugarcane bagasse-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for CIP degradation remains largely unexplored. Additionally, the influence of anatase-rutile phase composition in TiO₂ synthesized via a microwave-assisted approach using TiCl₃ as a precursor has not been systematically investigated. This gap necessitates the development of a scalable, sustainable strategy to enhance TiO₂ photocatalytic efficiency and ensure effective antibiotic degradation.

To overcome these challenges, CQDs have been investigated as promising co-catalysts for TiO₂ due to their unique optical and electronic properties. CQDs exhibit strong light absorption, tuneable photoluminescence, and efficient electron transfer, which facilitate enhanced charge separation and broadened light absorption into the visible spectrum [8,9]. The integration of CQDs with TiO₂ has been shown to suppress electron-hole recombination and improve photocatalytic performance. The synthesis of CQDs from agricultural waste has gained traction as a sustainable and cost-effective alternative to conventional synthesis methods. Sugarcane bagasse, an abundant byproduct of the sugar industry, serves as an excellent precursor for CQD production due to its high carbon content and renewability. Previous studies have explored biomass-derived nanomaterials such as Na₂SiO₃-activated porous carbon from bamboo biomass, biochar from pomegranate peels, and activated carbon from sugarcane bagasse for wastewater treatment and pollutant degradation. Previous study developed a bio-based ceramic/organic xerogel from sugarcane bagasse for antibiotic adsorption [10]. However, limited research has focused on the photocatalytic degradation of CIP using sugarcane bagasse-derived CQDs. This highlights the need for an innovative approach that leverages sustainable CQD-TiO₂ composites for antibiotic removal from wastewater.

Microwave-assisted synthesis has emerged as a rapid, energy-efficient, and scalable technique for CQD production, offering several advantages over conventional hydrothermal methods [9,11]. These include shorter reaction times, precise control over particle size, and improved quantum yields. Additionally, microwave synthesis enables uniform incorporation of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, which enhances charge transfer and extends light absorption into the visible spectrum [11–13]. Another critical factor in photocatalytic performance is the phase composition of TiO₂. Studies have shown that the anatase-rutile mixed phase structure significantly enhances charge separation and improves photocatalytic activity [14,15]. However, the potential of microwave-assisted synthesis using TiCl₃ as a precursor to achieve controlled phase composition remains underexplored. This provides an opportunity to optimize TiO₂ phase junctions for superior charge transport while maintaining scalability and cost-effectiveness.

This study aims to develop a facile and sustainable microwave-assisted synthesis method for CQDs derived from sugarcane bagasse and integrate them with TiO₂ synthesized using TiCl₃ as a precursor to achieve controlled anatase-rutile phase composition. The CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were comprehensively characterized to evaluate their structural, optical, and electrochemical properties. A comprehensive photocatalytic investigation was conducted to assess CIP degradation under UV light, identifying the key reactive species involved in the degradation process. Furthermore, LC-MS/MS analysis was performed to confirm the complete mineralization of CIP and provide insights into its degradation pathway. This research demonstrates the synergistic effect of CQDs and the anatase-rutile TiO₂ interface in optimizing photocatalytic efficiency. By employing microwave-assisted synthesis, this work establishes a scalable, cost-effective route for designing advanced photocatalysts for wastewater treatment. The findings provide valuable insights into the degradation pathways of CIP and contribute to the development of green, high-performance photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

Sugarcane bagasse (SB) was sourced from a local sugarcane juice vendor in the Khon Kaen university area, Thailand. The raw SB was thoroughly washed with deionized water, sun-dried for up to seven days, and subsequently ground into smaller fragments using a blender. Citric acid (C₆H₈O₇), aqueous ammonia solution (28–30 % NH₃), titanium trichloride (TiCl₃, 10–15 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), silver nitrate (AgNO₃), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), Ciprofloxacin (CIP), an HPLC-grade secondary standard (98 % purity, traceable to USP 1134313), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from sugarcane bagasse
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized via a firstly introduced microwave-assisted method from sugarcane bagasse applied from the hydrothermal approach described by Pandiyan et al., (2020). CQDs were synthesized utilizing a microwave-assisted (MW) approach. In brief, varying amounts of sugarcane bagasse (250, 500, 750, and 1000 mg) were combined with 500 mg of citric acid (CA) in 25 mL of deionized water. Ammonia (NH₃) was subsequently added to adjust the pH of the mixture to 7 under magnetic stirrer, resulting in a homogeneous solution. This solution was then irradiated using a domestic microwave at power of 950 W, 760 W, and 470 W for durations of 1, 3, 5, and 10 min, respectively. Subsequently, the solution was sonicated for 30 min to facilitate further dispersion, and then filtered through filter paper to obtain the CQDs.

TiO₂ was synthesized using a facile microwave-assisted method, developed by the irradiation of a power input at 170 W, with TiCl₃ as the precursor. A 10–15 % TiCl₃ solution (40 mL) was prepared by mixing the precursor with an equal volume of deionized water under vigorous stirring. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was added to adjust the pH of the solution to 7. The synthesis process utilized a domestic microwave operating at 170 W for 7 min, ensuring a rapid and efficient reaction. The resulting solid product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with an acidic solution (pH 1) and deionized water to eliminate any residual salts.

CQDs/TiO₂ NCs was synthesized by microwave-assisted method. A specific volume of CQDs were first dispersed in 5 mL of deionized water. For different loadings of 5 % v/v, 10 % v/v, and 20 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, CQDs were pipetted 0.5 mL, 1 mL, and 3 mL, respectively. Each mixture was then combined with 0.1 g of TiO₂ in 5 mL of deionized water, resulting in a final volume of 10 mL. The solution was then sonicated for 30 min to ensure proper dispersion. The solution was then subjected to microwave irradiation at 950 W for 2 min to facilitate the formation of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs.

The photocatalytic degradation of CIP was evaluated by preparing a 50 mL solution of CIP (20 mg/L) in scintillation glass vials and exposing it to UV light (150 W, 280 nm) for 5 h. The UV lamp was positioned at 20 cm from the sample. Prior to illumination, the CIP solution and catalyst were kept in the dark for 60 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Control experiments were conducted in the absence of a catalyst to account for any non-catalytic degradation. Photocatalytic performance was assessed using 50 mg of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, 50 ppm of CQDs, and 50 mg of pristine TiO₂.

The degradation of CIP was monitored by measuring the UV–Vis absorption spectra at a maximum wavelength of 278 nm using a Shimadzu UV-1800 UV/Visible Scanning Spectrophotometer. After the photocatalytic reaction, the catalyst was recovered by centrifugation at 10.500 rpm for 10 min. The catalyst was reused in five consecutive cycles to evaluate its stability and photocatalytic performance degradation over time. The degradation pathway was investigated using LC-MS/MS (Xevo TQ-XS Triple Quadrupole, Waters, UK). The details condition of LC-MS/MS was shown in Table S3.

The scavenger test was performed to identify the key reactive species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. IPA, EDTA, and AgNO₃ were used as scavengers to quench hydroxyl radicals (•OH), holes (h+), and electrons (e−), respectively. The concentrations of the scavengers were set at 10 mM for IPA and 5 mM for both EDTA and AgNO₃. A 1 M stock solution of each scavenger was prepared, from which 0.5 mL of IPA and 0.25 mL each of EDTA and AgNO₃ were added to a 50 mL solution containing CIP and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs. The mixture was stirred to ensure homogeneity and subsequently exposed to UV light for 5 h. The impact of each scavenger on the photocatalytic process was analysed to elucidate the dominant reactive species contributing to CIP degradation.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent response measurements were conducted to evaluate the charge transfer dynamics and photoelectrochemical properties of the catalyst. TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were deposited onto indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass, which served as the working electrode. A platinum electrode was used as the counter electrode, while an Ag/AgCl electrode functioned as the reference electrode. All measurements were performed in a 0.5 M Na₂SO₄ electrolyte solution to ensure sufficient ionic conductivity and system stability.

For the preparation of the working electrode, 6.3 mg of PVDF was dissolved in 2.5 mL of NMP and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, 2.5 mg of the catalyst was introduced into the solution, followed by continuous stirring for 24 h to achieve uniform dispersion. The resulting suspension was then deposited the ITO glass substrate in 20 μL increments and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h to ensure proper adhesion and stability.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized catalysts were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-1800 UV–Vis Scanning Spectrophotometer. The crystal structure of the TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs was analysed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a PANalytical EMPYREAN instrument with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.545 Å) over a diffraction angle range of 0–80°. The morphology and size of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Talos F200x G2, Thermo Scientific, Netherlands) operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEOL JSM-6010LV) operating at 20 kV. SEM was coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) for elemental analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using an AXIS ULTRADLD system (Kratos Analytical, Manchester, UK) with a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source (1.4 keV). The XPS chamber was maintained at a base pressure of approximately 5 × 10−9 Torr. A hemispherical analyzer detected the photoelectrons at a 90° angle to the sample surface. The oxidation states and chemical environments of elements on the surface of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were examined using high-resolution XPS spectra. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted on the CQDs derived from sugarcane bagasse using a Bruker TENSOR 27 spectrometer to identify functional groups present on the surface of the CQDs. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out using a Jasco Spectrofluorometer FP-8050 Series with excitation at 320 nm using a Xenon lamp. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and transient photocurrent response was performed to further investigate the charge transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface using CS series electrochemical workstation (Wuhan corrtest instrument Corp, Ltd., China). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and porosity measurements were conducted using a Micromeritics SmartVac Prep, with the catalyst undergoing degassing at 150 °C.

UV–Vis spectroscopy was employed to investigate the optical properties of CQDs, TiO2, and CQDs/TiO2 NCs. The spectra of CQDs (Fig. 1a) exhibited two distinct absorption peaks at 242 nm and 335 nm. The 242 nm peak was attributed to the π–π* electronic transition of graphitic aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon atoms (C=C), while the 335 nm peak, associated with surface states on the co-functionalized CQDs, was attributed to n–π* transitions induced by functional groups such as NH₂, C-OH, and CO at the edges of the carbon core. These results were consistent with previous studies [11,16,17]. The diluted CQD solution appeared light yellow under visible light but exhibited blue luminescence when exposed to UV light (365 nm), indicating its fluorescence properties.

The intensity of the peaks varied with the sample preparation. The sample synthesized with 500 mg precursor mass for 10 min exhibited the highest absorbance, indicating enhanced graphitic structure or a higher concentration of surface functional groups. In contrast, the sample prepared with 500 mg precursor mass for 3 min showed lower intensity, suggesting less efficient formation of CQDs. The sample synthesized with 1000 mg precursor mass and a short reaction time (3 min) exhibited the lowest intensity, likely due to incomplete or non-uniform formation of CQDs caused by precursor aggregation at higher concentrations. This aggregation may have suppressed the optical characteristics of the CQDs, with insufficient time for the formation of well-defined structures, thus reducing active sites for light absorption [18].

The UV–Vis spectra of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (Fig. 1b) showed a noticeable redshift in the absorption edge compared to pure TiO₂ NPs. This redshift was attributed to the chemical interactions between TiO₂ and CQDs via the formation of Ti-O-C bonds [19,20]. These interactions introduce additional electronic states within the bandgap of TiO₂, facilitating electronic transitions at lower energy levels. These states could arise from quantum confinement effects, surface functional groups, or defect states in CQDs. Moreover, the CQDs enhanced the interfacial transport of electron-hole (e−/h+) pairs, thereby improving the photocatalytic efficiency of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs.

The bandgap values were determined using the Tauc plot. The bandgap of pure TiO₂ (Fig. 1c inset), measured at 3.4 eV, confirmed its wide-bandgap semiconductor nature, which limits its activity to UV light. However, the bandgap of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (Fig. 1c) was reduced to 3.1 eV, which is consistent with previous study [21,22]. This reduction indicated that the incorporation of CQDs into TiO₂ enhanced its optical properties, shifting absorption into the visible light region. The narrowing of the bandgap was attributed to up-conversion photoluminescence and electronic interactions facilitated by CQDs, which also improved photocatalytic activity. The adsorption of CQDs onto TiO₂ leads to significant hybridization between the conduction band (CB) of TiO₂ and the π-electrons in the aromatic rings of the CQDs, enhancing light absorption. Additionally, new energy states were introduced into the lower CB, effectively reducing the bandgap and improving the photocatalytic properties of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs compared to pure TiO₂.

The PL spectra of CQDs synthesized under varying conditions of bagasse carbon matrix mass, microwave power levels, and irradiation times were investigated to optimize their properties for photocatalytic applications. These parameters were optimized to achieve the desired optical properties for CQDs, which were critical for the integration of CQDs into CQDs/TiO₂ NCs for photocatalysis. The optimal conditions for CQDs fabrication were achieved with a carbon matrix mass of 500 mg, an irradiation time of 10 min, and a microwave power level of 950 W, resulting in a quantum yield (QY) of 58.09 %. The QY of the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 2a) was measured using quinine sulfate (QS) as the standard, and the results were summarized in Table S1. Further details on the synthesis conditions and additional results were provided in the supplementary information. The PL spectra was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The spectra exhibited a characteristic blue fluorescence, attributed to quantum effects, the increased surface area, and the presence of emissive traps. Notably, the fluorescence properties were significantly enhanced when CA and NH₃ were added to the synthesis process, highlighting the importance of these reagents in tuning the emission characteristics of CQDs.

Among the three microwave power levels tested, 950 W yielded the highest PL intensity, identified as the optimal condition for CQD synthesis. Other conditions, such as 500 mg carbon matrix for 10 min, 500 mg for 5 min, and 1000 mg for 3 min, produced progressively lower intensities (Figure S1-S3). The PL emission was primarily attributed to the functional groups present on the CQDs, with a reduction in peak area corresponding to a decrease in the density of these groups. This emission spectrum was linked to intrinsic photoluminescence from the π* (sp2) to π transition at sp2 sites [23].

A clear distinction had been observed between the PL spectra of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, as shown in Fig. 2b. The strongest emission peak at 365 nm, corresponding to the band transition of TiO₂, had been slightly shifted compared to previous reports around 360 nm [24]. The peak at 397 nm had typically been associated with band-gap transitions in TiO₂, resulting from the recombination of e− and h+ at the conduction and valence bands, respectively. Additionally, the emissions at 421, 450, 468, 483, and 494 nm had been attributed to defect states within the TiO₂ crystal lattice, particularly oxygen vacancies and surface imperfections [25]. These defects had facilitated visible light absorption, which might have enhanced the photocatalytic performance of the material.

A significant reduction in PL intensity had been observed for CQDs/TiO₂ NCs compared to pure TiO₂, indicating a decrease in charge carrier recombination due to the successful incorporation of CQDs. Among the CQDs/TiO₂ NC samples, the 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs had exhibited the lowest PL intensity, signifying their superior ability to suppress electron-hole recombination. This had suggested that the 10 % CQD loading had provided an optimal balance, promoted efficient electron transfer and enhanced photocatalytic efficiency. In contrast, 5 % CQDs/TiO₂ NCs had shown a lower PL intensity than pure TiO₂ but remained higher than that of the 10 % CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, indicating a moderate improvement in charge separation. However, the 20 % CQDs/TiO₂ NCs had exhibited a higher PL intensity compared to pure TiO₂, suggesting that excessive CQD loading had led to increased recombination, likely due to aggregation effects hindering charge transport pathways. This behaviour had confirmed that in pure TiO₂, photogenerated electrons had tended to recombine rapidly, limiting its photocatalytic efficiency [22]. However, upon the introduction of CQDs onto the TiO₂ surface, excited electrons had transferred from TiO₂ into the CQD structure, thereby enhancing charge separation and suppressing recombination, ultimately improving photocatalytic performance. These findings had demonstrated that CQDs had played a crucial role in modulating charge carrier dynamics, and the 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs had provided the most favourable conditions for efficient charge transfer, making them the most promising photocatalyst for environmental applications.

The XRD pattern of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were presented in Fig. 3a and b, respectively, to provide insights into their structural and phase compositions. For the synthesized TiO₂ (Fig. 3a), distinct diffraction peaks corresponding to the anatase phase were identified at 2θ values of 25.3° (101), 37.8° (004), and 48.0° (200). Minor contributions from the rutile phase were also observed at 27.4° (110), 36.1° (101), and 41.2° (210). These phase assignments were matched with the JCPDS Card No. 21–1272 for anatase and JCPDS Card No. 21–1276 for rutile [20]. The co-existence of both anatase and rutile phases was confirmed, with anatase identified as the dominant crystalline structure.

For the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (Fig. 3b), additional peaks at lower 2θ values, notably at ∼11° (001) and ∼ 21° (002), were observed and attributed to the successful incorporation of CQDs into the TiO₂ matrix. A broader peak in the 20°–30° range further supported the formation of CQDs, in agreement with previous studies [22,26,27]. The primary anatase peaks were retained, indicating that the microwave-assisted conjugation of CQDs did not induce any phase transformation of TiO₂, preserving the photocatalytic properties of the anatase structure. The crystallinity values were determined based on the method described in this paper [28], yielding 88.28 %, 80.55 % for TiO₂, and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, respectively. The decrease in crystallinity upon CQDs incorporation suggests that CQDs may introduce lattice distortions or inhibit full crystallization, leading to a more disordered structure. This reduction could be attributed to surface interactions between CQDs and TiO₂, which create defects or amorphous regions.

However, a decrease in the intensity of certain diffraction peaks in the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs was observed, which was attributed to a reduction in crystallinity caused by the microwave-assisted incorporation of CQDs. This reduction likely resulted from the uniform dispersion of CQDs within the TiO₂ matrix, which induced minor lattice distortions during synthesis. Additionally, slight shifts in peak positions were observed, suggesting strong electronic interactions between the CQDs and TiO₂, or minor lattice strain introduced by the CQDs. These interactions were considered critical as they potentially altered the electronic structure of the composite, enhancing charge carrier mobility and dynamics at the CQDs-TiO₂ interface. Furthermore, the hybridization between the CB of TiO₂ and the π-electrons of CQDs may have introduced localized energy states, promoted visible light absorption and extended the photoresponse range. These findings demonstrate the successful integration of CQDs into the TiO₂ matrix, improving photocatalytic efficiency by facilitating charge transfer and light-harvesting properties, despite slight reductions in crystallinity.

The success of the microwave-assisted synthesis in producing CQDs and TiO₂ was confirmed through TEM and SEM analyses, as shown in Figs. 4 . The TEM image of the CQDs (Fig. 4a) revealed uniformly distributed spherical particles with an average size of 3.0 nm, as indicated by the inset histogram. Lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.208 nm were observed in the inset, corresponding to the (100) plane of the CQDs, confirming their graphitic structure. These findings were consistent with previous reports [22,26,29] The homogeneous dispersion of CQDs within the composite was further validated through TEM-EDX mapping.

The SEM image of TiO₂ (Fig. 4b) illustrated aggregated nanoparticles. The particle size distribution inset showed that the average size was 62.1 nm, which was significantly larger than the CQDs, indicating that robust TiO₂ nanostructures had been formed. These structures were considered essential for photocatalytic applications. In the HR-TEM image of TiO₂ (Fig. 4c), clear lattice fringes were identified, with interplanar spacings of 0.237 nm and 0.356 nm corresponding to the (004) and (101) planes of anatase, respectively, while a spacing of 0.169 nm was assigned to the (211) plane of rutile TiO₂ [24,30,31]. The coexistence of anatase and rutile phases was confirmed, which was expected to enhance photocatalytic efficiency through interphase charge separation.

In Fig. 5 , structural and compositional details of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were highlighted. Lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.349 nm and 0.244 nm were observed, corresponding to the (101) planes of anatase and rutile TiO₂ phases, respectively. The red circles in the image indicated that CQDs were well dispersed and integrated into the TiO₂ matrix, emphasizing their nanoscale interaction. The HAADF (High-Angle Annular Dark Field) image demonstrated that the CQDs were uniformly distributed within the composite. Elemental mapping analysis verified the successful integration of carbon (yellow), oxygen (red), and titanium (blue)within the TiO₂ framework, with each element distinctly mapped, providing clear evidence of homogeneous CQD distribution throughout the nanocomposite. Additionally, EDX spectra with atomic weight distributions (Figure S6) further confirmed the elemental composition and uniform dispersion of the incorporated components. These findings validated the coexistence of anatase and rutile phases in TiO₂, while also confirming that CQDs were uniformly distributed, enhancing interfacial interactions and charge transport efficiency within the nanocomposite.

FTIR spectroscopy was employed to investigate the functional groups and chemical composition of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs. The FTIR spectra of these materials were shown in Fig. 6 , with a comparative analysis provided for sugarcane bagasse, the carbon matrix, and CQDs in Figure S4. The FTIR spectrum of sugarcane bagasse exhibited a broad peak in the range of 2986.56–3642.69 cm−1, indicative of hydroxyl groups associated with cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin (Table S2). Other characteristic peaks included C-OH stretching at 1034.78 cm−1 (cellulose), CO stretching at 1638.74 cm−1 (lignin), and aromatic skeletal vibrations between 1241.11 and 1364.43 cm−1, ascribed to lignin. Peaks at 2353.36 cm−1 and 2898.02 cm−1 corresponded to CH and -CH₂ stretching, respectively. These results confirmed that sugarcane bagasse is primarily composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin [32,33].

During the carbonization process, sugarcane bagasse was transformed into a more oxidized carbon matrix. The FTIR spectrum of the carbon matrix showed a reduction in the intensity of the C-OH peak (945.45–1310.67 cm−1) due to dehydration reactions, which resulted in the removal of hydroxyl groups through the expulsion of water molecules. A prominent new peak at 1596.84 cm−1, corresponding to CO stretching, indicated an increase in oxygenated functional groups. Additionally, OH vibrations were observed between 3679.84 and 3768.68 cm−1, suggesting the formation of a condensed structure with fewer hydroxyl groups. The absence of the aromatic peak in the carbon matrix spectrum indicated the breakdown of lignin's aromatic structures during carbonization.

The FTIR spectrum of CQDs revealed peaks at 654.54 cm−1 (CC), 1641.11 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1239.52 (CN stretching), 3382.0 (NH), and 2879.05–3683.00 cm−1 (OH stretching). These peaks confirmed the presence of carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups in CQDs, which were critical for enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. The FTIR spectrum of TiO₂ exhibited characteristic peaks between 639.53 and 896.43 cm−1, corresponding to O-Ti-O vibrations, and a peak at 1599.11 cm−1, attributed to TiO vibrations. Additional peaks at 1733.59 cm−1 and 2352.42 cm−1 were assigned to CO stretching, while peaks at 3686.16 cm−1 and 3784.98 cm−1 corresponded to Ti3+-OH vibrations [11,19,24,34]. For CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, the FTIR spectrum confirmed the successful integration of CQDs into TiO₂. Key features included the retention of O-Ti-O and TiO peaks at 639.53–896.43 cm−1 and 1599.11 cm−1, respectively, along with the appearance of CO stretching at 1733.59 cm−1 and 2352.42 cm−1. The peaks corresponding to Ti3+-OH vibrations (3686.16–3784.98 cm−1) remained present, indicating that the hybridization process did not alter the structural integrity of TiO₂. These observations, summarized in Table S2, demonstrate the successful conjugation of CQDs with TiO₂, enhancing photocatalytic activity through improved light absorption, charge separation, and surface reaction efficiency.

The XPS analysis of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs provided significant insights into its elemental composition and chemical states. In the Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 7a), prominent peaks at 458.8 eV and 464.5 eV were attributed to Ti4+ 2p₃/₂ and Ti4+ 2p₁/₂, respectively, indicating that the valence state of titanium remained unchanged after the deposition of CQDs onto TiO₂. These findings were consistent with previous studies [9,22,27]. Smaller peaks at 457.6 eV and 463.3 eV were also observed, suggesting the presence of Ti3+ species, which were indicative of oxygen vacancies or surface defects in the TiO₂ lattice [35].

A comparison of XPS spectra between CQDs/TiO₂ and pure TiO₂ (Figure S5) highlighted significant differences arising from the incorporation of CQDs. In the Ti 2p spectra, CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited peaks at 459.2 eV (Ti4+, Ti 2p₃/₂) and 464.9 eV (Ti 2p₁/₂), along with additional contributions from Ti3+ at 457.9 eV and 463.8 eV, indicating partial reduction of Ti4+ due to interactions with CQDs. In contrast, pure TiO₂ primarily showed peaks for Ti4+ at 458.7 eV and 464.5 eV. The observed shift, indicated a strong interaction between the CQDs and TiO₂, potentially altering the surface states of TiO₂. The presence of reduced titanium species was a characteristic feature of many titania-based photocatalysts.

In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 7b), peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.6 eV were attributed to CC, CN, CO, and C=O–Ti bonds, respectively. The presence of the C=O–Ti peak confirmed the interaction between CQDs and TiO₂, validating the successful formation of the composite. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 7c), nitrogen doping within the CQDs was evident from peaks at 398.0 eV, 399.0 eV, 400.3 eV, 401.7 eV, and 403.4 eV, corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen (C=N–C), pyrrolic nitrogen (NH), graphitic nitrogen (C₃–N), quaternary nitrogen, and oxidized nitrogen, respectively [9,22,27,36,37].

The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5d) displayed peaks at 530.1 eV, 531.2 eV, 532.0 eV, and 533.1 eV, which were assigned to TiO, OC, OC groups, and OH species, respectively. The presence of OH groups and Ti3+ species indicated an increase in surface reactivity, which may contribute to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the composite. Overall, the XPS results confirmed the successful integration of CQDs into the TiO₂ matrix. The identified chemical interactions, oxygen vacancies, and surface functionalities were crucial for improving the photocatalytic efficiency of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs.

The surface area and porous structure of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were analysed using N₂ adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution, as illustrated in Fig. 8 , while Table S4 presented the corresponding textural properties. According to IUPAC classification, the materials exhibited Type-IV isotherms with H₂ hysteresis loops (Fig. 8a), confirming their mesoporous nature [19,38]. A gradual decrease in adsorption capacity was observed with increasing CQD content, suggesting that the introduction of CQDs modified the pore structure of TiO₂. The pore size distribution curves (Fig. 8b) revealed that both TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs maintained a relatively narrow pore size distribution, with variations influenced by CQD incorporation.

The BET surface area analysis confirmed a significant impact of CQDs on TiO₂ porosity. Pure TiO₂ had the highest BET surface area of 259.89 m2/g, indicating a well-developed porous framework. With 5 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, the surface area slightly decreased to 245.97 m2/g, implying partial pore filling. A more pronounced reduction was measured at 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (204.50 m2/g), suggesting that CQDs occupied and modified the mesoporous structure, potentially enhancing charge separation. The lowest surface area was obtained for 20 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (147.85 m2/g), indicating that excessive CQD loading led to aggregation and further pore blockage, reducing available adsorption sites [39,40].

The BJH desorption average pore width of pure TiO₂ was determined at 3.97 nm. A slight reduction was recorded in 5 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (3.91 nm) and 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs (3.89 nm), suggesting that CQDs partially filled the pores. However, an increase to 5.13 nm in 20 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs indicated structural rearrangement or changes in pore connectivity due to high CQD loading. The observed trends suggest that CQD incorporation altered the mesoporous structure of TiO₂, reducing surface area and modifying pore size distribution. The 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the most favourable balance between surface area and porosity, which could enhance photocatalytic efficiency by improving charge separation while maintaining sufficient active sites for reaction.

The interfacial charge transfer kinetics and charge separation efficiency of TiO₂ and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were evaluated using EIS and transient photocurrent response measurements, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . The Nyquist plots (Fig. 9a) reveal distinct differences in charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) among the samples. Pure TiO₂ exhibited the largest semicircle, indicating high R
ct and poor electron transport, which is attributed to rapid charge recombination and limited conductivity. Upon CQD incorporation, R
ct decreased, confirming that CQDs acted as electron acceptors, promoting charge separation and enhancing electron mobility. The 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the smallest semicircle, signifying the lowest Rct and the most efficient charge transfer. This result suggests that at 10 % CQD loading, the presence of CQDs significantly reduced charge recombination and improved electron transport pathways. However, a further increase in CQD content to 20 % v/v resulted in an enlarged semicircle, indicating an increase in R
ct . This suggests that excessive CQD loading led to aggregation, which created charge trapping sites that hindered electron mobility rather than enhancing it.

The transient photocurrent response measurements (Fig. 9b) further validate the EIS results, providing direct evidence of charge carrier dynamics. 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the highest photocurrent response, indicating enhanced charge separation and prolonged charge carrier lifetime. This confirms that an optimal CQD loading improves charge transport efficiency while minimizing recombination. 5 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited a moderate photocurrent response, suggesting an improvement in charge separation, though not as pronounced as the 10 % v/v sample. Conversely, 20 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the lowest photocurrent response, even lower than pure TiO₂, indicating that excessive CQDs hindered charge mobility, likely due to excessive surface coverage or aggregation blocking active sites. These EIS and transient photocurrent response findings were consistent with previous [20,38].

These results align well with the PL and BET analyses, further reinforcing the conclusion that 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs provide the best balance between charge transport, surface area, and recombination suppression. The lowest PL intensity observed in 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs confirmed efficient charge separation, while the BET analysis indicated a well-balanced porosity, ensuring that CQD incorporation did not excessively reduce the available surface area for reaction. The superior performance of 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs in both EIS and transient photocurrent response measurements confirms that this composition offers the most favourable electronic and structural properties for photocatalysis. Based on these findings, 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs were selected for photocatalytic performance evaluation due to their optimal charge transfer efficiency, minimal recombination, and well-maintained surface area, making them the most promising composition for enhancing photocatalytic activity.

Photodegradation kinetic studies were conducted for various catalysts under identical conditions. Prior to light exposure, an aqueous suspension containing CIP was stirred vigorously in darkness for 30 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. As shown in Fig. 10a, the degradation of CIP followed a pseudo-first-order reaction. Among the tested catalysts, the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the highest degradation rate, significantly outperforming individual CQDs, TiO₂, and the control without a catalyst.

The CIP degradation kinetics, shown as the C/C₀ ratio over time in Fig. 10b, demonstrated that the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs achieved the fastest degradation rate, with nearly 100 % degradation efficiency attained within 300 min (Fig. 10c). This exceptional performance was attributed to the synergistic effects between CQDs and TiO₂, as confirmed by XPS analysis. The XPS results revealed the presence of Ti3+ species and oxygen vacancies in TiO₂, both of which were essential for enhancing charge carrier separation and improving surface reactivity, thereby promoting the photocatalytic process.

The CQDs used in this study were doped with nitrogen via a simple NH₃ treatment during synthesis, as verified by FTIR and XPS analysis. The nitrogen doping introduced pyridinic and graphitic nitrogen species, significantly enhancing the electronic properties of CQDs by increasing electron density and facilitating the migration of photoinduced charge carriers. This doping strategy not only improved the conductivity of CQDs but also created additional active sites for redox reactions, contributing to the overall photocatalytic efficiency.

Furthermore, the interaction between N-CQDs and TiO₂ at the interface led to the formation of C=O–Ti bonds, which facilitated efficient charge transfer and reduced recombination losses. The oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs were also found to enhance the adsorption of CIP molecules onto the catalyst surface, accelerating the degradation process. The decreased PL intensity observed for CQDs/TiO₂ NCs relative to TiO₂ further supports the notion of improved charge separation and reduced recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. This behaviour, as indicated by the PL analysis, directly correlated with the superior photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs. These combined effects enhanced charge separation, facilitated charge transfer, and improved adsorption of CIP molecules were responsible for the significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs compared to pure TiO₂.

The reusability of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs photocatalyst was evaluated over five consecutive cycles of CIP photodegradation (Fig. 10d). After each cycle, the photocatalyst was recovered, rinsed with deionized water, dried, and reused. A significant decline in photocatalytic efficiency was observed, with degradation efficiencies decreasing to 67.89 % for CQDs/TiO₂ NCs and 63.57 % for TiO₂ after the fifth cycle. This reduction in performance was attributed to the potential loss of active catalyst material during recycling process and the deactivation of surface-active sites, possibly due to the accumulation of reaction intermediates or byproducts. Additionally, CQDs were found to bleach over time when subjected to prolonged UV light exposure [41]. Despite this, the structural integrity of the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs was maintained, indicating its chemical stability and robustness.

The scavenger test was conducted to elucidate the reaction mechanism and determine the primary reactive species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP using CQDs/TiO₂ NCs under UV irradiation. IPA, EDTA, and AgNO₃ were introduced as selective scavengers for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), photogenerated holes (h+), and electrons (e−), respectively. The corresponding degradation efficiencies are presented in Fig. 11 .

The control experiment, performed in the absence of scavengers, exhibited an exceptionally high degradation efficiency of 99.91 %, confirming the strong photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs. When IPA was introduced to quench •OH radicals, the degradation efficiency decreased to 70.36 %, indicating that •OH played a role in CIP degradation, though it was not the primary reactive species. A more pronounced suppression was observed in the presence of EDTA, where the degradation efficiency drastically dropped to 15.82 %, suggesting that photogenerated h+ was the dominant oxidative agent. The most significant inhibition occurred in the presence of AgNO₃, which reduced the degradation efficiency to 7.46 %, confirming that electron transfer was essential for sustaining the reaction by facilitating charge separation and reactive species formation.

These findings were aligned with the well-established role of •OH radicals in photocatalysis, where they were reported to exhibit a high electrode potential of 2.73 V vs. NHE, making them highly reactive in the breakdown of organic contaminants [42]. Within photocatalytic systems such as TiO₂, •OH radicals were continuously generated as photogenerated h+ oxidized surface OH− or H₂O, ensuring a sustained production of reactive species. While •OH contributed to overall degradation, the scavenger test confirmed that h+-driven oxidation was the dominant mechanism in the CQDs/TiO₂ NC system. Additionally, •OH radicals played a secondary role in re-oxidizing surface-active sites, which helped maintain catalyst activity and improved charge transfer efficiency.

The sharp decline in degradation efficiency with AgNO₃ was observed, confirming that while electrons were not the primary oxidative species, their movement was crucial for charge separation and sustaining the photocatalytic reaction. These results highlighted the synergistic interaction between CQDs and the controlled anatase-rutile TiO₂ phase, which enhanced charge carrier dynamics and overall photocatalytic performance. The study provided deeper insights into the reaction mechanism, demonstrating the potential of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs as an effective photocatalyst for environmental remediation, particularly in the degradation of antibiotic contaminants in wastewater.

As summarized in Table 1 , a remarkable CIP removal efficiency of 99.91 % under UV light within 300 min was demonstrated by the CQDs/TiO₂ photocatalyst, outperforming many previously reported catalysts. This superior photocatalytic activity was ascribed to the enhanced charge separation, suppressed recombination rates, and extended light absorption resulting from the incorporation of N-CQDs. The electronic properties of CQDs were improved by nitrogen doping from ammonia, which also contributed to their chemical functionality, facilitating the adsorption and degradation of CIP molecules. Although a significant decrease in activity over multiple cycles was observed, highlighting the need for further optimization of catalyst recovery and regeneration strategies, the high initial efficiency and reasonable stability of CQDs/TiO₂ were demonstrated, indicating its potential for practical wastewater treatment applications.

The degradation of CIP after 300 min of irradiation in the presence of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs as the photocatalyst was investigated using LC-MS/MS, with proposed degradation pathways. The LC-MS/MS spectra indicated the fragmentation pattern of CIP, with the parent ion observed at 360 m/z (Fig. 12 ). As the degradation progressed, peaks corresponding to smaller molecular ions appeared, suggesting the sequential loss of functional groups and the formation of intermediate degradation products.

The degradation of CIP in the presence of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs was predominantly driven by oxidative processes induced by UV irradiation, was primarily directed at the piperazine group, resulting in the oxidation of CIP. The first step involved the cleavage of the piperazine ring at the nitrogen atom of the secondary aliphatic amine group, which generated smaller fragments. This was followed by the substitution of the fluorine atom and the breakdown of the quinolone backbone. This degradation pattern, including the removal of the carboxyl group and subsequent fragmentation of the piperazine structure, was consistent with previous studies [50–52].

The degradation pathway of CIP was found to follow two primary routes, as depicted in Fig. 13 . In the first pathway, the oxidation of the piperazine ring led to its cleavage at the nitrogen atom, producing smaller molecular fragments. This pathway further involved the loss and oxidation of the aliphatic amine group, fluorine substitution, and degradation of the quinolone backbone. These processes led to the formation of smaller ions observed at m/z values of 135, 107, and 58, indicating the breakdown of the piperazine ring and quinolone backbone. The second pathway began with the cleavage of the carboxylic acid group, which produced intermediates at 288 m/z. This step was followed by the fragmentation of the quinolone ring, including the removal or substitution of the fluorine atoms. The resulting degradation products were further broken down into smaller ions, confirming the sequential and stepwise nature of the degradation process.

When compared to other photocatalytic systems, such as pure TiO₂ and unmodified CQDs, the CQDs/TiO₂ NCs demonstrated superior degradation efficiency. The CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited faster cleavage of the piperazine ring and more pronounced breakdown of the quinolone backbone. This enhanced performance was attributed to the synergistic effects of the CQDs, which facilitated charge separation and enhanced the oxidative degradation of CIP.

The enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Anatase-Rutile TiO₂ NCs was attributed to a synergistic interaction between CQDs and the mixed-phase TiO₂ structure, effectively improving charge separation and charge carrier dynamics. As illustrated in Fig. 14 , the proposed mechanism integrates findings from PL, BET, EIS, transient photocurrent response, and scavenger test results, providing a comprehensive understanding of the enhanced photocatalytic activity.

Under UV irradiation, electrons in the VB of TiO₂ were excited to the CB, generating electron-hole pairs. Simultaneously, CQDs absorbed light, promoting electrons from their π orbital to the higher energy π* orbital, leading to photoexcited electrons in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of CQDs. These electrons were transferred from the LUMO of CQDs to the CB of TiO₂, which effectively facilitated charge separation and reduced electron-hole recombination. This was supported by PL analysis, which demonstrated the lowest recombination rate in CQDs/TiO₂ NCs.

The EIS and transient photocurrent response results further validated this mechanism by showing that CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance and highest photocurrent density, confirming efficient charge transport and enhanced carrier lifetime. The photogenerated holes in the VB of TiO₂ were transferred to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of CQDs, preventing recombination and enabling efficient hole-driven oxidation [53]. This was supported by the scavenger test results, where CIP degradation was primarily suppressed in the presence of EDTA, confirming that h+ played the dominant role in oxidative reactions. The separated holes in CQDs actively participated in oxidation processes, facilitating CIP degradation.

The anatase-rutile TiO₂ mixed-phase structure further contributed to enhanced charge separation and photocatalytic activity through favourable band alignment. At the anatase/rutile interface, the CB of anatase was higher than that of rutile, while the VB of rutile was higher than that of anatase [54–56], enabling efficient charge migration and spatial charge separation. Under UV irradiation, both anatase and rutile phases were photoactivated, generating electron-hole pairs. The photoexcited electrons in the anatase CB migrated to the lower-energy CB of rutile, reducing recombination probability. Simultaneously, holes in the rutile VB transferred to the higher-energy VB of anatase, further promoting charge separation. CQDs played a crucial role as electron mediators, improving charge separation and electron transport efficiency. Electrons in the rutile CB were transferred to the anatase CB, and subsequently to the LUMO of CQDs, where they facilitated the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by interacting with dissolved oxygen. These ROS actively degraded CIP, consistent with the observed charge mobility enhancement in EIS results. Meanwhile, holes in the anatase VB migrated to the HOMO of CQDs, preventing recombination and participating in oxidation reactions. Additionally, holes in the rutile VB reacted with hydroxide ions (OH−) and water molecules, generating hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which further contributed to CIP degradation [54,57,58]. This mechanism was supported by scavenger tests, where IPA addition suppressed degradation, confirming that •OH participated in the photocatalytic reaction but was secondary to hole-driven oxidation.

The nitrogen doping in CQDs, as confirmed by XPS and FTIR analysis, introduced intermediate energy states within the TiO₂ bandgap, which extended light absorption into the visible region. The presence of pyridinic and graphitic nitrogen promoted electron density redistribution, accelerating charge migration and enhancing photocatalytic efficiency. Additionally, nitrogen-based functional groups facilitated stronger adsorption of CIP molecules, improving pollutant degradation efficiency. BET analysis revealed an optimized pore structure in 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, which further contributed to enhanced pollutant adsorption and reaction kinetics.

This synergistic mechanism was amplified by the incorporation of CQDs, which enhanced light absorption, extended spectral response into the visible range, and improved charge migration at the TiO₂ interface. The anatase-rutile phase junction effectively suppressed electron-hole recombination, while CQDs contributed to enhanced pollutant adsorption and efficient charge transport, leading to superior photocatalytic performance. The 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs, having demonstrated optimal charge separation, high photocurrent response, lowest charge transfer resistance, and balanced surface area, were selected as the most effective photocatalyst. These findings reinforce the critical role of CQDs and the anatase-rutile TiO₂ interface in enhancing charge carrier dynamics, reducing recombination, and ultimately improving photocatalytic performance for environmental remediation applications.

This study successfully synthesized CQDs, TiO₂, and CQDs/TiO₂ NCs using a microwave-assisted method, offering a rapid, sustainable, and eco-friendly approach with high quantum yield. The synthesized TiO₂ featured a mixed anatase-rutile phase, which played a crucial role in enhancing photocatalytic efficiency by facilitating efficient charge separation and improved electron mobility. The incorporation of CQDs significantly boosted the photocatalytic performance, achieving an exceptional CIP degradation efficiency of 99.91 % under UV light, far surpassing pristine TiO₂. The composite also exhibited notable stability, retaining 69 % of its photocatalytic efficiency after five consecutive cycles, demonstrating its potential for practical wastewater treatment applications. However, further optimization of structural integrity and durability is necessary to extend its long-term usability.

The mechanistic investigation revealed that CQDs/TiO₂ NCs effectively degraded CIP into smaller, less toxic byproducts, confirming their strong mineralization capability. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was primarily driven by synergistic effects, including efficient charge separation, extended light absorption enabled by N-doped CQDs, and accelerated electron transfer facilitated by the anatase-rutile phase junction. The EIS and transient photocurrent response measurements confirmed that 10 % v/v CQDs/TiO₂ NCs exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance and highest photocurrent density, establishing this composition as the most effective photocatalyst. Moreover, PL analysis validated that CQDs effectively suppressed charge recombination, further contributing to performance enhancement.

This study underscores the scientific significance of CQDs/TiO₂ NCs as high-performance photocatalysts, highlighting their potential for wastewater treatment and environmental remediation. The suitability of the microwave-assisted synthesis method was demonstrated, offering a scalable and energy-efficient route for developing advanced photocatalytic materials. The findings provide a valuable framework for future research, particularly in exploring strategies to enhance long-term stability, extend visible-light absorption, and optimize CQD loading for improved pollutant degradation. Future studies should also focus on integrating CQDs/TiO₂ NCs into real wastewater systems, evaluating their performance under varying environmental conditions, and developing hybrid photocatalytic approaches to further enhance their practical applicability. This research paves the way for sustainable and highly efficient solutions to address the global challenge of water pollution.
